summary: Joel Spolsky traces the history of VBA from its origins as Excel's macro language through its strategic role as a lock-in mechanism for Microsoft Office. He explains how Microsoft deliberately built VBA and its complex object model as a barrier to entry that competitors couldn't perfectly emulate. The irony, he argues, is that Microsoft's decision to drop VBA from Mac Office 2008 undermines their own lock-in strategy, forcing loyal users to reconsider their platform choices. He draws parallels to the VB6-to-VB.Net transition and the Windows XP-to-Vista upgrade, arguing that Microsoft has abandoned the backwards-compatibility philosophy that made them dominant. Spolsky concludes that breaking your own lock-in features is self-defeating when your business depends on upgrade revenues.
key_points: VBA was designed not just as a user convenience but as a strategic lock-in mechanism, since competitors could never perfectly emulate the vast, undocumented API surface
Dropping VBA from Mac Office forces users to reevaluate their platform choice entirely, turning Microsoft's own lock-in into an exit catalyst
Microsoft has abandoned its backwards-compatibility religion across Office, Windows, and Basic simultaneously, threatening three core businesses that depend on upgrade revenue
API emulation is fundamentally brittle — subtle undocumented behaviors mean you don't get partial credit when trying to replicate a large programming interface
Each recent Microsoft release requires hacks and workarounds just to maintain prior functionality, creating upgrade fatigue among loyal users
money_quotes: In the brittle world of programming, such imperfections often mean your program crashes long before it does anything useful. You don't get partial credit when you try to emulate an API.

Microsoft has managed to hoist itself by its own petard. By locking in users and then not supporting their own lock-in features, they're effectively making it very hard for many Mac Office 2004 users to upgrade.
When Microsoft lost the backwards-compatibility religion that had served them so well in the past, they threatened three of their most important businesses.

Much as professional programmers sneer at the Basic programming language, market research unambiguously showed that about 2/3rds of the kinds of accidental programmers who develop macros preferred Basic to other languages and perceived it to be easy.
Despite complicated technical explanations, every development decision like this is based on a cost/benefit analysis.

It is extremely frustrating how much you have to run in place just to keep where you were before with Microsoft's products, where every recent release requires hacks, workarounds, and patches just to get to where you were before.
key_insight: When a platform vendor abandons backwards compatibility with the very lock-in features that kept users captive, they don't free users — they push them toward competitors.
